channel.title: Texts on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Texts on emsenn.net

item.title: Research Sources and Citations
item.description:
This document provides detailed citations for all technical standards, regulations, and research referenced in this knowledge domain.

Standards Documents

HEPA Filter Classification

EN 1822-1:2019 (ISO 29463-1:2021)

  • High Efficiency Air Filters and Filter Media for Removing Particles from Air
  • European standard / International standard harmonization
  • Defines H10–H14 classification based on efficiency at Most Penetrating Particle Size (MPPS, ~0.21 μm)
  • Specifies testing procedures for each class
  • H13 and above receive individual test certificates with assigned efficiency values
  • Current reference: ISO 29463 (supersedes older EN 1822-1 terminology but maintains classification equivalence)
  • Source: EN1822 HEPA Filter Testing Standard — Smart Air

US DOE Standard (DOE-STD-3020-2005)

item.title: Technical Foundations of Compact HEPA Purification
item.description:
Portable HEPA air purifiers are evaluated using multiple independent standards and certifications. Understanding these standards—HEPA classification, CADR measurement, ACH calculation, carbon adsorption, AHAM verification, and CARB compliance—provides the foundation for assessing purifier design and selecting devices appropriate for specific applications.

HEPA Filter Standards: EN 1822 vs. DOE

The fundamental specification is the HEPA filter itself. Two major standards define HEPA performance:

The European standard EN 1822 (now ISO 29463) classifies HEPA filters by their efficiency at the Most Penetrating Particle Size (MPPS), approximately 0.21 micrometers. This size is chosen because it represents the weakest point in filtration: too small for efficient interception or impaction, too large for diffusion. EN 1822 establishes a hierarchy from H10 (85% at MPPS) through H14 (99.995% at MPPS), with H13 and above receiving individual test certificates.
